item.title: “Good sirs, if, while reflecting, a monk sees clearly that all unarisen evil, unwholesome states are not yet abandoned within him, then he should arouse…
item.description:
“Good sirs, if, while reflecting, a monk sees clearly that all unarisen evil, unwholesome states are not yet abandoned within him, then he should arouse energy to abandon those evil, unwholesome states. Good sirs, if, while reflecting, a monk sees clearly that all evil, unwholesome states have been abandoned within him, then he should dwell with joyful mind, training in wholesome states day and night.”

From: Majjhima Nikāya 15 – Anumānasutta
📖 Read this sutta

item.title: “Monk, when a person dwells with sense faculties guarded, with moderation in eating, committed to wakefulness, watching over wholesome states, devoted to developing the factors…
item.description:
“Monk, when a person dwells with sense faculties guarded, with moderation in eating, committed to wakefulness, watching over wholesome states, devoted to developing the factors of enlightenment at the beginning and end of the night — then his body does not feel intoxicated, his eye sees directions clearly, the Dhamma is remembered by him. Sloth and torpor do not overcome his mind. With gladness, he lives the holy life. He has no doubts about wholesome states. Therefore, monks, you should train thus: ‘I will guard the sense faculties … be devoted to developing the factors of enlightenment at the beginning and end of the night.’”

From: Aṅguttara Nikāya 5.56
📖 Read this sutta

item.title: “And how, monks, is a monk one who does not bind up the wound? Here, monks, when a monk sees a form with the eye,…
item.description:
“And how, monks, is a monk one who does not bind up the wound? Here, monks, when a monk sees a form with the eye, he grasps the general feature, grasps the particular feature. Those causes whereby, if the eye faculty were not restrained, covetousness, sorrow, evil, unwholesome states would arise—he does not restrain that cause, does not guard the eye, does not practice restraint of the eye. When he hears a sound with the ear … smells an odor with the nose … tastes a flavor with the tongue … feels a touch with the body … cognizes a phenomenon with the mind, he grasps the general feature, grasps the particular feature. Those causes whereby, if the mind faculty were not restrained, covetousness, sorrow, evil, unwholesome states would arise—he does not restrain that cause, does not guard the mind, does not practice restraint of the mind. Thus, monks, is a monk who does not bind up the wound.”

From: Majjhima Nikāya 33 – Mahāgopālakasutta
📖 Read this sutta

item.title: “And what, monks, are the things that should be fully understood? Form, monks, is a thing that should be fully understood; feeling … perception ……
item.description:
“And what, monks, are the things that should be fully understood? Form, monks, is a thing that should be fully understood; feeling … perception … formations … consciousness is a thing that should be fully understood. These, monks, are the things that should be fully understood. And what, monks, is full understanding? The destruction of greed, the destruction of hatred, the destruction of delusion — this, monks, is called full understanding.”

From: Saṃyutta Nikāya 22.23
📖 Read this sutta

item.title: “Monks, there are these four ignoble searches. What four? Here, monks, a person being himself subject to aging seeks what is subject to aging; being…
item.description:
“Monks, there are these four ignoble searches. What four? Here, monks, a person being himself subject to aging seeks what is subject to aging; being himself subject to sickness seeks what is subject to sickness; being himself subject to death seeks what is subject to death; being himself subject to defilement seeks what is subject to defilement. These, monks, are the four ignoble searches.”

From: Aṅguttara Nikāya 4.255
📖 Read this sutta

item.title: “Having gone forth, Anuruddhas, what should a clansman do? Anuruddhas, secluding himself from sensual desires, secluding himself from unwholesome states. If he does not attain…
item.description:
“Having gone forth, Anuruddhas, what should a clansman do? Anuruddhas, secluding himself from sensual desires, secluding himself from unwholesome states. If he does not attain rapture and pleasure or some other state more peaceful than that, then sensual lust invades his mind and remains; ill-will … sloth and torpor … restlessness and remorse … doubt … discontent … laziness invades his mind and remains. Anuruddhas, when he has not secluded himself from sensual desires, from unwholesome states, he does not attain rapture and pleasure or some other state more peaceful than that. Anuruddhas, when he does seclude himself from sensual desires, from unwholesome states, he attains rapture and pleasure or some other state more peaceful than that; then sensual lust does not invade his mind and remain; ill-will … sloth and torpor … restlessness and remorse … doubt … discontent … laziness does not invade his mind and remain. Anuruddhas, when he has secluded himself from sensual desires, from unwholesome states, he attains rapture and pleasure or some other state more peaceful than that.”

From: Majjhima Nikāya 68 – Naḷakapānasutta
📖 Read this sutta

item.title: “If there were no gratification in form, monks, beings would not become lustful for form. But because there is gratification in form, beings become lustful…
item.description:
“If there were no gratification in form, monks, beings would not become lustful for form. But because there is gratification in form, beings become lustful for form. If there were no peril in form, beings would not become disenchanted with form. But because there is peril in form, beings become disenchanted with form. If there were no escape from form, beings would not escape from form. But because there is escape from form, beings escape from form. Monks, as long as beings do not directly know as it actually is the gratification as gratification, the peril as peril, the escape as escape in regard to these six internal sense bases, they have not, monks, along with this world of devas, Māras, Brahmās, with its ascetics and brahmins, with its devas and humans, dwelt with minds free from clinging, detached, liberated, unbounded. But because beings have directly known them as they actually are, therefore they have dwelt with minds free from clinging, detached, liberated, unbounded.”

From: Saṃyutta Nikāya 35.18
📖 Read this sutta

item.title: The nun Alavikā, knowing “That is Māra,” recited this verse: There is an escape in the world — I have realized it through wisdom. O…
item.description:
The nun Alavikā, knowing “That is Māra,” recited this verse:

There is an escape in the world —
I have realized it through wisdom.
O heedless relative (Māra),
How could you know that Dhamma?
Sensual desires are like swords and spears,
The aggregates are a chopping block.
What you call sensual pleasure,
I call no pleasure at all.

From: Saṃyutta Nikāya 5.1
📖 Read this sutta

item.title: “There is, monks, an unborn, unbecome, unmade, unconditioned. If there were not that unborn, unbecome, unmade, unconditioned, no escape would be discerned from the born,…
item.description:
“There is, monks, an unborn, unbecome, unmade, unconditioned. If there were not that unborn, unbecome, unmade, unconditioned, no escape would be discerned from the born, become, made, conditioned. But because there is an unborn, unbecome, unmade, unconditioned, therefore escape is discerned from the born, become, made, conditioned.”

That which is born, become, arisen,
Made, conditioned, impermanent,
Composed of aging and death,
A nest of disease, perishable,
Born from nutriment and craving —
That is not fit to delight in.

But the escape from that —
Peace, beyond reasoning,
Unborn, unbecome,
The sorrowless state, the path without defilement,
The cessation of painful states,
The stilling of conditioned things —
That is happiness.

From: Itivuttaka 43
📖 Read this sutta

item.title: “If he feels a pleasant feeling, he understands that it is impermanent, he understands that it is not to be clung to, he understands that…
item.description:
“If he feels a pleasant feeling, he understands that it is impermanent, he understands that it is not to be clung to, he understands that it is not to be delighted in. If he feels a painful feeling … If he feels a neither-painful-nor-pleasant feeling, he understands that it is impermanent, he understands that it is not to be clung to, he understands that it is not to be delighted in. If he feels a pleasant feeling, he feels it with a mind free from attachment. If he feels a painful feeling … If he feels a neither-painful-nor-pleasant feeling, he feels it with a mind free from attachment.”

From: Saṃyutta Nikāya 12.51
📖 Read this sutta
